metadata.description: Marketplace of Claude Code / Cowork plugins for preparing U.S. court documents, organized one plugin per state, plus a shared data-only plugin for federal law. Seven state plugins today: wa-court-docs (Washington — GR 14-compliant pleadings, KCDC + KCSC + the populous-counties roll-up, RCW 19.16 / WA CPA consumer-debt defense bundle), or-court-docs (Oregon — UTCR 2.010-compliant pleadings, Multnomah + Washington County Circuit Court specifics + the populous-counties roll-up, ORS 697 / UTPA consumer-debt defense bundle), ca-court-docs (California — CRC 2.100-2.119-compliant pleadings, LASC + SFSC + populous-counties roll-up, Rosenthal Act / FDBPA / CDCLA / UCL consumer-debt defense bundle), co-court-docs (Colorado — C.R.C.P. 10 + CJD 11-01-compliant pleadings, Denver + Arapahoe + populous-counties roll-up, CFDCPA / CCPA / UCCC consumer-debt defense bundle, plus a UDMA-based family-law bundle), in-court-docs (Indiana — Ind. Trial R. 5(E) pleadings under Indiana Trial Rules with the Jarboe burden-shifting and T.R. 59 motion-to-correct-error quirks, Marion Superior Court / Lake Superior Court + populous-counties roll-up, IUCCC / DCSA consumer-debt defense bundle leaning on chain-of-title + FDCPA since Indiana has no collection-agency licensing regime), and ny-court-docs (New York — 22 NYCRR § 202.5 paper format + 22 NYCRR § 202.5-b NYSCEF electronic-filing format with the New York caption and "-against-" party separator, five flagship Supreme Court venues each as its own skill — New York County (1st JD, Manhattan, Commercial Division at the $500k threshold), Kings County (2nd JD, Brooklyn, with CPLR 3408 foreclosure conferences), Bronx County (12th JD), Nassau County (10th JD, Mineola, Commercial Division at $200k), and Queens County (11th JD, Jamaica) — plus dedicated venue skills for the rest of NY's fragmented civil-court system — ny-nyc-civil-court (Civil Court of the City of New York; Civil Court Act $50k cap, 5 boroughs, the highest-volume consumer-debt forum in the country), ny-nyc-housing-court (Housing Part; RPAPL Article 7 summary proceedings; Local Law 136 universal Right to Counsel; ERAP stays), ny-nassau-dc + ny-suffolk-dc (Long Island District Courts under UDCA / 22 NYCRR Part 212), ny-city-courts (~60 upstate City Courts under UCCA / 22 NYCRR Part 210, $15k cap), ny-justice-courts (~1,250 Town and Village Justice Courts under UJCA / 22 NYCRR Part 214, $3k cap), and ny-family-court (FCA Articles 3-10; CSSA; family-offense Orders of Protection; abuse and neglect) — plus a long-tail Supreme Court roll-up (ny-county-courts) for Suffolk / Westchester / Erie / Monroe / Onondaga Supreme Court Civil Term; **five subject-matter bundles**: ny-consumer-debt (FDCPA / Reg F / 2022 CCFA / GBL § 600 / GBL § 349 / CPLR 4544 / UCC Article 9 chain of title); ny-landlord-tenant (RPAPL Article 7 / 2019 HSTPA / 2024 Good Cause Eviction / RPL § 235-b / Local Law 136 / ERAP); ny-personal-injury (CPLR Article 14-A pure comparative fault + Article 16, Insurance Law § 5102(d) no-fault threshold, Labor Law § 240(1) scaffold law, GML § 50-e Notice of Claim, CPLR 214-a medical malpractice, CVA / ASA revival windows); ny-employment (NYS HRL, NYC HRL with *Williams* construed-broadly rule, Labor Law § 191/198/740 + WARN, CROWN Act); ny-commercial-disputes (22 NYCRR § 202.70 Commercial Division + Appendix A; CPLR 3016(b) fraud particularity; BCL § 720 / § 1104-a derivative + dissolution; Faithless Servant; GOL §§ 5-1401 / 5-1402 destination-forum)). All six state plugins depend on the shared claude-legal-federal-laws plugin for FDCPA / FCRA / TILA / ECOA / EFTA / Garnishment + Reg B-F-V-Z-DD-E-M-N-P-X + RESPA + SCRA + FHA + FTC Telemarketing Sales Rule + Title 11 Bankruptcy + model UCC content — declared in their plugin.json and dereferenced from in-tree symlinks at install time, so the federal corpora live in one canonical place. All state plugins are architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills plus subject-matter bundles. The structure leaves clean slots for plugins covering additional states. The seventh plugin, oh-court-docs (Ohio — Ohio Civ. R. 10 + per-court local rules, 8 flagship Court of Common Pleas venues + county-courts roll-up + oh-municipal-courts + oh-family-court, R.C. Chapter 1345 CSPA consumer-debt defense bundle + R.C. Title 31 + Title 33 family-law bundle).

plugins.name: claude-legal-federal-laws
plugins.description: Shared reference corpus for federal U.S. debt-collection and consumer-finance law (FDCPA, FCRA, TILA, ECOA, Reg B/F/V/Z) plus the model Uniform Commercial Code (Articles 1, 2, 3, 9). Data-only — no skills, no slash commands. State plugins declare this as a dependency; the marketplace runtime auto-installs it and dereferences the symlinks each state plugin uses to point into this directory.

plugins.name: wa-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, declarations, motions, notes for motion docket, and proposed orders for Washington courts across the full civil-practice surface. Applies GR 14 formatting; provides dedicated venue skills for King County District Court (wa-kcdc), King County Superior Court (wa-kcsc), Pierce County Superior Court / Tacoma (wa-pierce), Snohomish County Superior Court / Everett (wa-snohomish), Spokane County Superior Court (wa-spokane), the Superior Court Family Law Department (wa-family-court), and a roll-up for the rest of the state (wa-county-courts). Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (CR + GR + ER + 35 court-rule sets, fees and costs, local rules, citation format, online sources, discovery, first-30-days response, hearings, filing packets, post-judgment, fact-checking, deadlines, drafting scaffolders) plus six subject-matter bundles: wa-consumer-debt (FDCPA, Reg F, RCW 19.16, Washington CPA, chain of title), wa-family-law (Washington's community-property regime + RCW Title 26 dissolution / parenting / child support framework + the 2022 consolidated RCW 7.105 civil-protection-order regime + RCW 26.26A UPA parentage), wa-landlord-tenant (RCW 59.18 RLTA + the 2019 SB 5600 mandatory-form pay-or-vacate reform + the 2021 SB 5160 just-cause framework + HB 1815 statewide tenant Right to Counsel + ERP), wa-personal-injury (RCW 4.22 pure comparative fault + several-liability post-1986 Reform Act + WPLA at RCW 7.72 + medical malpractice at RCW 7.70 + Notice of Tort Claim under RCW 4.92 / 4.96), wa-employment (Minimum Wage Act + WLAD at RCW 49.60 + PFML under RCW Title 50A + non-compete reform at RCW 49.62 + L&I-exclusive workers' comp at RCW 51), and wa-commercial-disputes (Washington CPA at RCW 19.86 with the Hangman Ridge 5-element test + WBCA at RCW 23B + LLC Act at RCW 25.15 + UCC at RCW 62A + MAR under RCW 7.06). Follows a **thin-skill architecture**: SKILL.md bodies describe procedural frameworks and point at the references corpus for current statutory text, dollar thresholds, day counts, and section subsections rather than embedding them — so quarterly corpus refreshes update the canonical law without requiring skill edits. Reference corpora include 35 court-rule sets covering 1,233+ rules and 92 RCW chapters covering 3,255+ sections, plus the shared federal-debt-laws and ucc-model content via the claude-legal-federal-laws dependency. All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.

plugins.name: or-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, declarations, motions, notes for hearing, and proposed orders for Oregon circuit courts. Applies UTCR 2.010 statewide formatting; includes Multnomah County Circuit Court (Portland) and Washington County Circuit Court (Hillsboro) specifics plus a county-court roll-up for the other most-populous counties (Clackamas, Lane, Marion, Jackson, Deschutes, Linn, Benton, Yamhill, Polk, Douglas); supports pro se workflows. Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (ORCP civil rules, OEC evidence rules, ORS 20 fees and costs, local SLR rules, citation format per the Oregon Appellate Courts Style Manual, online sources, discovery, first-30-days response, hearings, filing packets, post-judgment, fact-checking, deadlines under ORCP 10 + ORS 187.010 holidays) plus subject-matter bundles that plug into the procedural skills. The first subject-matter bundle, or-consumer-debt, covers FDCPA, Reg F, ORS 697 (Collection Agency Registration), the Oregon Unlawful Trade Practices Act (UTPA, ORS 646.605 et seq.), chain-of-title doctrine, fact-pattern triage, and synthetic example filings for debt-buyer defense. The architecture leaves clean slots for future bundles (landlord-tenant under ORS 90, family, personal injury, etc.). All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.

plugins.name: ca-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, declarations, motions, notices of motion, and proposed orders for California superior courts. Applies California Rules of Court 2.100-2.119 statewide formatting; includes Los Angeles Superior Court (LASC — Stanley Mosk Courthouse) and San Francisco Superior Court (SFSC — Civic Center Courthouse, Dept. 302 law-and-motion) specifics plus a county-court roll-up for the other most-populous counties (Orange, San Diego, Riverside, San Bernardino, Santa Clara, Alameda, Sacramento, Contra Costa, Fresno, Kern); supports pro se ('In Pro Per') workflows. Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (CCP civil rules with the 35-interrogatory cap and 45-day motion-to-compel-further deadline, CEC evidence rules, ORS 20-style California fee-and-cost framework under CCP §§ 1032/1033.5/§ 1717, local rules for LASC + SFSC + populous counties, citation format per the California Style Manual, CRC 8.1115 unpublished-opinion rule, deadline arithmetic under CCP §§ 12/12a/12c with Govt. Code § 6700 holidays, hearings under the CRC 3.1308 tentative-ruling regime) plus subject-matter bundles that plug into the procedural skills. The first subject-matter bundle, ca-consumer-debt, covers FDCPA, Reg F, the Rosenthal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788-1788.33), the Fair Debt Buying Practices Act (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1788.50-1788.66), the California Debt Collection Licensing Act (Cal. Fin. Code §§ 100000-100027), the Unfair Competition Law (Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 17200-17210), the Consumers Legal Remedies Act (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1750-1784), chain-of-title doctrine under Cal. Comm. Code Art. 9, fact-pattern triage, and synthetic example filings for debt-buyer defense. All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.

plugins.name: co-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, declarations, motions, notices, and proposed orders for Colorado courts. Applies C.R.C.P. 10 + Chief Justice Directive 11-01 statewide formatting (uniform 1-inch margins, double-spaced 12-point text, the two-block Colorado caption with the case-number / division / courtroom box, Colorado attorney-registration number for licensed counsel under C.R.C.P. 11(a)); includes Denver District Court (2nd Judicial District — Lindsey-Flanigan Courthouse) and Arapahoe County District Court (18th Judicial District — Centennial, Aurora, Littleton) specifics plus a county-courts roll-up for the other most-populous counties (Jefferson, El Paso, Adams, Boulder, Larimer, Douglas, Weld, Pueblo, Mesa, Broomfield); supports pro se / self-represented workflows including CCEFS Pro Se and the Colorado Judicial Branch JDF (Judicial Department Forms) catalog. Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (C.R.C.P. civil rules with the 21-day answer window under Rule 12(a)(1) and the C.R.C.P. 121 § 1-15 motion-practice timing — 21-day response, 7-day reply, 15-page motion / 10-page reply limits, certificate of conferral under § 1-15(8); CRE evidence rules; fees and costs under C.R.S. art. 16 of title 13; local rules and Chief Justice Directives; Colorado neutral-citation conventions for post-2012 cases at the Colorado Supreme Court and Court of Appeals; deadline arithmetic under C.R.C.P. 6 with C.R.S. § 24-11-101 holidays including Frances Xavier Cabrini Day (replaced Columbus Day in 2020 via H.B. 20-1031) and Juneteenth; hearings under the Cisco Webex statewide remote-appearance protocol with division-by-division practice standards; first-30-days response under C.R.C.P. 12 including the Twombly/Iqbal-equivalent pleading standard adopted in Warne v. Hall, 2016 CO 50; post-judgment under C.R.C.P. 59/60 and the C.R.S. § 13-54.5 garnishment regime with the expanded SB22-086 exemption table including the $250,000 homestead) plus subject-matter bundles. Two subject-matter bundles ship: (1) co-consumer-debt covering FDCPA, Regulation F, the Colorado Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (CFDCPA — C.R.S. art. 16 of title 5, recodified from Title 12 in 2022 by HB22-1024), the Colorado Consumer Protection Act (CCPA — C.R.S. art. 1 of title 6 with treble damages and mandatory fees under C.R.S. § 6-1-113, subject to the Hall v. Walter 'significant public impact' element), the Uniform Consumer Credit Code (UCCC — C.R.S. art. 1-9 of title 5 administered by the Colorado AG), Colorado collection-agency licensure under C.R.S. § 5-16-115 and the AG's Collection Agency Board, chain-of-title doctrine under Colorado UCC Article 9 (C.R.S. art. 9 of title 4) including the bill-of-sale-and-assignment foundation, the 6-year SOL on liquidated debt under C.R.S. § 13-80-103.5(1)(a) as settled in Hassler v. Account Brokers of Larimer Cnty., the 5-pattern fact-pattern triage (stale credit-card debt, debt buyer with original-creditor contract, medical-debt collection under HB23-1126, default-judgment attack under C.R.C.P. 60(b), FDCPA/CCPA counterclaim package), and synthetic example filings; and (2) co-family-law covering the Uniform Dissolution of Marriage Act (UDMA — C.R.S. art. 10 of title 14) with the 91-day residency requirement under § 14-10-106(1)(a)(I) and the 91-day waiting period under § 14-10-106(1)(a)(III), dissolution of marriage / legal separation under § 14-10-106, declaration of invalidity of marriage (annulment) under § 14-10-111 with the narrow grounds (lack of capacity, impotence, underage, bigamy, prohibited-by-law, jest), C.R.C.P. 16.2 mandatory financial-disclosure regime including the Sworn Financial Statement (JDF 1111) due 42 days after service, allocation of parental responsibilities (replaced 'custody' terminology in 1998) under § 14-10-124 with decision-making and parenting-time allocation, the C.R.S. § 14-10-115 income-shares child-support guideline with the 93-overnight rule and the statewide online worksheet (JDF 1820 E) and imputation of income under § 14-10-115(5)(b), modification of child support requiring a substantial-and-continuing change of >= 10% under § 14-10-122(1)(b), maintenance under the C.R.S. § 14-10-114 guideline framework as amended in 2014 and 2024, common-law marriage under People v. Lucero (1987) as modernized by In re Marriage of Hogsett & Neale, 2021 CO 1, and UCCJEA jurisdiction over children at C.R.S. art. 13 of title 14. All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.

plugins.name: in-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, declarations, motions, notices, and proposed orders for Indiana courts. Applies Indiana Trial Rule 5(E) formatting (Indiana lacks a single consolidated format rule like GR 14 / UTCR 2.010 / CRC 2.100; the formatting requirements live across T.R. 5(E), T.R. 11, and county local rules); covers Marion Superior Court (Indianapolis, statewide's highest civil-case-volume court — 36 numbered courtrooms in the City-County Building) and Lake Superior Court (Crown Point, with Hammond and East Chicago branches in Indiana's second-largest county), plus a populous-counties roll-up (Allen, Hamilton, St. Joseph, Vanderburgh, Elkhart, Tippecanoe, Porter, Madison); supports pro se workflows under the *Goossens v. Goossens* same-standard rule. Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (Indiana Trial Rules — incl. T.R. 6 time, T.R. 12 motions, T.R. 26-37 discovery with T.R. 33(A)'s 25-interrogatory cap, T.R. 56 summary judgment under the *Jarboe v. Landmark* affirmative-negation standard, the unique T.R. 59 motion-to-correct-error procedure, T.R. 60 relief), Indiana Rules of Evidence (IRE — incl. 803(6) business records, 902(11) self-authentication), Indiana Code citation conventions, fees and costs under IC 33-37, local rules for Marion (LR49) and Lake (LR45), IC 1-1-9-1 holiday-calendar deadline arithmetic (including Indiana-specific Good Friday and even-year primary/general election days), Odyssey statewide e-filing) plus subject-matter bundles. The first subject-matter bundle, in-consumer-debt, covers FDCPA, Reg F, the Indiana Uniform Consumer Credit Code (IUCCC, IC 24-4.5), the Deceptive Consumer Sales Act (DCSA, IC 24-5-0.5 — Indiana's UDAP/UTPA analog with treble damages + fees on uncured deceptive acts), chain-of-title doctrine under Indiana UCC Article 9 (IC 26-1-9.1), the four debt SOLs at IC 34-11-2-7/-9/-11/-13, and the fact that Indiana does NOT require collection-agency licensing (a distinct posture from Oregon's ORS 697 and California's CDCLA — defense focus shifts to chain of title + FDCPA + DCSA). All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.

plugins.name: ny-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, affirmations, affidavits, motions, notices of motion, orders to show cause, and proposed orders for New York courts. Applies 22 NYCRR § 202.5 paper format and 22 NYCRR § 202.5-b NYSCEF electronic-filing format (1-inch margins, double-spaced 12-point text, the New York caption with the "-against-" party separator, the Index Number, the assigned Justice + Part designation); includes five flagship Supreme Court venues each as its own skill — New York County (1st JD, Manhattan, 60 Centre Street, with the $500,000-threshold Commercial Division under 22 NYCRR § 202.70), Kings County (2nd JD, Brooklyn, 360 Adams Street, with the high-volume foreclosure settlement conference part under CPLR 3408), Bronx County (12th JD, 851 Grand Concourse), Nassau County (10th JD, Mineola, with a $200,000-threshold Commercial Division), and Queens County (11th JD, Jamaica) — plus dedicated venue skills for the rest of NY's fragmented civil-court system — ny-nyc-civil-court (Civil Court Act $50k cap, 5 borough branches, the highest-volume consumer-debt forum in the country with 22 NYCRR Part 208 + § 208.6-a default scrutiny), ny-nyc-housing-court (Housing Part of NYC Civil Court — RPAPL Article 7 summary proceedings + Local Law 136 universal Right to Counsel + ERAP stays + rent regulation overlays), ny-nassau-dc and ny-suffolk-dc (Long Island District Courts each as a dedicated skill under UDCA and 22 NYCRR Part 212), ny-city-courts (~60 upstate City Courts under UCCA / 22 NYCRR Part 210, $15k cap), ny-justice-courts (~1,250 Town and Village Justice Courts under UJCA / 22 NYCRR Part 214, $3k cap; the only civil forum for eastern Suffolk County matters under $3k), and ny-family-court (FCA Articles 3-10 / 22 NYCRR Part 205 — CSSA child support, custody, family-offense Orders of Protection, abuse and neglect) — plus a long-tail Supreme Court roll-up (ny-county-courts) covering Suffolk / Westchester / Erie / Monroe / Onondaga / Richmond / Rockland / Albany / Orange / Dutchess / Saratoga / Oneida Supreme Court Civil Term; supports pro se / self-represented workflows including the post-2023 CPLR 2106 universal affirmation under penalty of perjury (L 2023, ch 559 ending the notary-bottleneck for pro se filers). Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (CPLR civil rules with the broadest "material and necessary" disclosure scope in the U.S. at CPLR 3101(a), the CPLR 3130/3133(b) 25-interrogatory cap, the 22 NYCRR § 202.20-f good-faith-conferral rule, CPLR 3211 pre-answer motion-to-dismiss practice with the strict (e) consolidation rule, the Individual Assignment System under 22 NYCRR § 202.3, Microsoft Teams remote hearings, the Guide to NY Evidence in lieu of a codified evidence code with CPLR Article 45 codified evidence including CPLR 4518 business records and CPLR 4544 small-print contracts; CPLR 2103(b)(2) 5-day mail-service rule longer than the federal/WA/OR 3-day rule; deadline arithmetic under CPLR 2103 with NY Gen. Constr. Law § 24 holidays including the distinctive Lincoln's Birthday (Feb 12) court closure and annual Election Day closure; the Notice of Motion / Order to Show Cause / cross-motion framework under CPLR 2214 and 2215 with the 8-day minimum service period and the 22 NYCRR § 202.8-b 25-page memo / 15-page reply limits; the verified-vs-unverified pleading distinction under CPLR 3020; the CPLR 5015(a)(1)-(5) motion-to-vacate framework with the 1-year clock; CPLR Article 52 enforcement of money judgments including restraining notice (5222), income execution (5231), property execution (5232), the Exempt Income Protection Act (CPLR 5222-a) with the $3,090 automatic-protection threshold and EJ-FOC-1 exemption form, and the 20-year SOL on money judgments at CPLR 211(b); 22 NYCRR § 202.48 settle-order procedure with the jurisdictional 60-day clock) plus subject-matter bundles. Five subject-matter bundles ship: (1) ny-consumer-debt covering FDCPA, Reg F, the **2022 Consumer Credit Fairness Act** (3-year SOL at CPLR 213(a), heightened pleading at CPLR 3015(e), heightened default-judgment evidence at 22 NYCRR § 202.27-a, additional notice mailing at CPLR 308(six)), N.Y. GBL § 600 et seq. collection-agency licensing, N.Y. GBL §§ 349 / 350 deceptive acts and false advertising, CPLR 4544 small-print-contract exclusion, chain of title under N.Y. UCC Article 9 with CPLR 4518 business-records foundation per *Bank of NY Mellon v. Gordon*, 171 AD3d 197 (2d Dept 2019), 5-pattern fact-pattern triage, and synthetic example filings; (2) ny-landlord-tenant covering RPAPL Article 7 summary proceedings (14-day rent demand under § 711(2), 30/60/90-day holdover notice scaled by tenancy under RPL § 226-c), the **2019 HSTPA** reforms (1-month security-deposit cap at GOL § 7-108(1-a), late-fee cap at RPL § 238-a, bilateral attorney's-fee shifting at RPL § 234, 6-year rent-overcharge lookback), the **2024 Good Cause Eviction Law** at RPL Article 6-A, rent regulation regimes (Rent Stabilization, Rent Control, Loft Law, NYCHA, Section 8, Mitchell-Lama), RPL § 235-b implied warranty of habitability, NYC right-to-counsel under Local Law 136 of 2017 at NYC Admin Code § 26-1301, the Emergency Rental Assistance Program (ERAP) automatic stay, and a comprehensive defenses + counterclaims catalog; (3) ny-personal-injury covering CPLR Article 14-A pure comparative fault + Article 16 several-liability cap on non-economic damages, Insurance Law § 5102(d) no-fault "serious injury" threshold + Article 51 PIP, Labor Law § 240(1) absolute scaffold-law liability + § 241(6) Industrial Code (12 NYCRR Part 23) + § 200 common-law negligence, GML § 50-e 90-day Notice of Claim against state actors, CPLR 214-a 30-month medical-malpractice SOL with continuous-treatment toll, CPLR 214-c discovery-rule toxic-tort SOL, EPTL § 5-4.1 wrongful death (2-year SOL), Child Victims Act (CPLR 214-g) and Adult Survivors Act (CPLR 214-j) revival windows, and the NY-unique Bill of Particulars practice at CPLR §§ 3041-3043; (4) ny-employment covering the NYS Human Rights Law (NY Exec Law § 296, all-size employer post-2019, "petty slights" harassment standard), the NYC Human Rights Law (NYC Admin Code § 8-107 with the *Williams v. NYCHA* construed-broadly rule, mandatory attorney's fees under § 8-502(g), caregiver / salary-history / stalking-victim protections), Labor Law § 191 frequency-of-pay (*Vega* late-payment liquidated damages), § 198 wage theft (6-year SOL, 100/200% liquidated damages), Labor Law § 740 whistleblower (post-2022 expansion + 2-year SOL), NYS WARN Act (50+ employees, 90-day notice), 2018 NYS Sexual Harassment Act, and the CROWN Act (race discrimination by hair); (5) ny-commercial-disputes covering 22 NYCRR § 202.70 Commercial Division (county-by-county thresholds $50k-$500k; Appendix A's 25-page memos, proportionality-in-discovery rule, designated counsel, accelerated adjudication), CPLR 3016(b) fraud particularity, CPLR 213(2) 6-year contract SOL + 213(8) 6/2-year fraud discovery rule, BCL § 720 derivative actions + BCL § 1104-a judicial dissolution with § 1118 buyout election, LLC Law § 702 dissolution, the Faithless Servant Doctrine (*Phansalkar*), GOL §§ 5-1401 / 5-1402 NY-as-destination-forum at $250k / $1M thresholds, and CPLR 5004 9% pre-judgment interest. All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.

plugins.name: oh-court-docs
plugins.description: Draft and format pleadings, declarations, motions, notices, and proposed orders for Ohio courts. Applies Ohio Civ. R. 10 + per-court local rules statewide formatting. Includes eight flagship Court of Common Pleas venues each as its own skill — Cuyahoga (Cleveland), Franklin (Columbus), Hamilton (Cincinnati), Summit (Akron), Montgomery (Dayton), Lucas (Toledo), Stark (Canton), and Butler (Hamilton, OH) — plus a county-courts roll-up for the other 80 Ohio counties, a dedicated `oh-municipal-courts` skill covering the Ohio Municipal Court layer (R.C. Chapter 1901; $15,000 civil cap; $6,000 small-claims division; the primary pro-se consumer-debt forum in most Ohio counties), and an `oh-family-court` skill covering both the Domestic Relations Division of Common Pleas (divorce + married-parent custody/support) and the Juvenile Division (unmarried-parent custody + child-support enforcement + abuse/neglect under R.C. Chapter 2151). Supports pro se workflows. Architected as matter-neutral civil-procedure skills (Ohio Civ. R. with the FRCP-style framework; Ohio Evid. R.; Ohio Rules of Superintendence; CPLR-analog deadline arithmetic per Civ. R. 6; Ohio public-domain citation format `YYYY-Ohio-NNNN` mandatory in appellate cases since 2002) plus two baseline subject-matter bundles. (1) oh-consumer-debt covering FDCPA, Reg F, the **Ohio Consumer Sales Practices Act** (R.C. Chapter 1345) with R.C. 1345.09 treble damages on willful violations and mandatory attorney's fees, chain of title under Ohio UCC Article 9 (R.C. Chapter 1309), and the Ohio statute-of-limitations regime (R.C. 2305.06 six-year written-contract SOL; R.C. 1345.10 two-year CSPA SOL); Ohio has NO collection-agency licensing regime. (2) oh-family-law covering divorce + dissolution + annulment + legal separation under R.C. Chapter 3105 with equitable distribution (R.C. 3105.171; NOT a community-property state); allocation of parental responsibilities under R.C. 3109; child support guideline at R.C. Chapter 3119 (income-shares model with $300,000 combined-income cap and 10% deviation threshold); spousal support under R.C. 3105.18; UCCJEA at R.C. Chapter 3127; UIFSA at R.C. Chapter 3115. All workflows are skills (no slash commands) so the agent invokes them automatically from natural-language requests.
